hook.scenario: Think of an AWS server scaling up (inlet) while memory leaks crash instances (outlet). Pipes & Cisterns is just Time & Work with negative efficiency.

mental_model.intuition: Inlets add work (+), outlets destroy work (-).
mental_model.description: The core logic is identical to Time & Work. The only difference is that a leakage or emptying pipe has a negative rate of work. Net rate = sum of all inlets minus sum of all outlets.

pattern_discovery.setup: Track the net filling rate when an inlet and an outlet work together.
cases: Fill pipe A takes 10 mins -> +1/10 per min
Empty pipe B takes 15 mins -> -1/15 per min
pattern_discovery.prompt: If the outlet were faster than the inlet, what would happen to the net rate?
pattern_discovery.rule: Add the rates of filling pipes and subtract the rates of emptying pipes. If net rate is negative, the tank empties rather than fills.

formula.statement: Net rate = sum(inlet rates) - sum(outlet rates). Time to fill = Total Capacity / Net rate. If net rate is negative, Time to empty = Total Capacity / |Net rate|. Inlet rate = 1/T_fill. Outlet rate = 1/T_empty. LCM method: assume capacity = LCM of all times; assign each pipe its units per minute with sign (+/-). Combined time = Capacity / Net rate.
formula.explain: If net rate is positive, the tank fills. If net rate is negative, the tank empties. For problems mixing known fill/empty times with known flow rates (liters/min), convert: Capacity = time x flow rate.

worked_example.problem: Pipe A fills a tank in 20 hours, Pipe B fills it in 30 hours, and Pipe C empties it in 15 hours. If all three are opened simultaneously when the tank is empty, how long will it take to fill?
steps: Use LCM method: Capacity = LCM(20, 30, 15) = 60 units.
Pipe A fills 60/20 = 3 units/hr (positive). Pipe B fills 60/30 = 2 units/hr (positive). Pipe C empties 60/15 = 4 units/hr (negative).

shortcuts.title: LCM Method with Negatives
shortcuts.trick: Assume tank capacity = LCM of all times. Treat emptying pipes as negative efficiency.
shortcuts.example: Inlet=10m, Outlet=15m. Capacity=30L. Inlet=+3L/m, Outlet=-2L/m. Net=+1L/m. Time = 30/1 = 30m.

common_mistakes.title: Ignoring the sign of outlets
common_mistakes.explanation: Treating emptying pipes as positive work leads to impossibly fast fill times. Always subtract outlet rates.

recall_questions.q: Pipe A fills in 4h, Pipe B empties in 6h. What is the net filling rate?
If the net rate is -1/5 per hour, what does it mean?
recall_questions.answer: The full tank will be completely emptied in 5 hours.

recall_questions.q: Can a tank ever fill if the outlet is faster than the inlet?
recall_questions.answer: No, the net rate would be negative, meaning the tank is always losing water.

oa_questions.question: Two pipes can fill a tank in 15 and 20 hours. A third pipe can empty it in 30 hours. If all three are opened, how long to fill the tank?
oa_questions.approach: LCM(15, 20, 30) = 60 units. Pipe1 = +4 units/hr, Pipe2 = +3 units/hr, Pipe3 = -2 units/hr. Net = 5 units/hr. Time = 60/5 = 12 hours.

oa_questions.question: A leak in the bottom of a tank can empty it in 8 hours. An inlet pipe fills water at 6 liters a minute. When both are open, the tank empties in 12 hours. Find the capacity of the tank.
oa_questions.approach: Leak takes 8h (-1/8 per hr). Let inlet take x hrs (+1/x per hr). Net = 1/x - 1/8 = -1/12. So 1/x = 1/8 - 1/12 = 1/24. Inlet fills in 24 hours. Capacity = 24 hrs x 60 min/hr x 6 L/min = 8640 L.
